one_line_takeaway: EUPG achieves formal, data-level (not model-level) privacy guarantees for forgotten records by protecting training data before training rather than perturbing model parameters after training, giving unlearning speedups of roughly 14-15x on neural networks and 2.5-2.7x on XGBoost versus full retraining while remaining model-agnostic and avoiding the severe utility collapse (94.70%->45.91% accuracy) that the certified baseline exhibited on CIFAR10/ResNet18.
key_finding: On MLP (Table 3), all methods (Retrain, SISA, Certified-SP, EUPG-k, EUPG-eps) reach similar post-unlearning utility and near-random post-unlearning MIA AUC across Adult/Heart/Credit, but EUPG's pre-unlearning MIA AUC/TPR@1%FPR were already substantially lower than Retrain/SISA (e.g. Adult: EUPG(k=30) before-unlearning MIA AUC 51.48 vs Retrain 55.49; TPR@1% 1.54 vs 3.78). On XGBoost (Table 4), EUPG(k=30) showed only a slight utility drop after unlearning while EUPG(eps=1) showed a larger drop especially on Heart (70.72->70.74 vs Retrain 72.43->72.45), attributed to boosted-tree residual fitting where post-unlearning fine-tuning only updates leaf values, not tree structure; both EUPG variants had markedly lower pre-unlearning MIA AUC/TPR than Retrain/SISA (e.g. Heart: EUPG(k=30) MIA AUC 51.51 vs Retrain 64.58). On CIFAR10/ResNet18 (Table 5), Certified-SP collapsed to 45.91% test accuracy after unlearning (from 94.70% before) at epsilon=1, attributed by the original Certified-SP authors to the curse of dimensionality in model-space DP; SISA fell from 87.34% to 69.19% after unlearning; EUPG(k=30) achieved the best post-unlearning accuracy at 85.40% and EUPG(eps=1) achieved 83.17%, both with superior pre-unlearning MIA resistance. On unlearning efficiency (Table 6), EUPG achieved 14.76x (k=30) and 14.84x (eps=1) average speedup over Retrain on neural networks, versus 14.04x for Certified-SP; on XGBoost, EUPG achieved 2.74x and 2.46x speedups (absolute times 0.030-0.048s) versus Retrain, while SISA was slower than full retraining in both model families (0.62x and 0.33x) because the randomly sampled 5% forget set forced all 5 SISA shards to retrain from slice 0.
core_contribution: The paper introduces EUPG (Efficient Unlearning with Privacy Guarantees), a machine-unlearning framework that shifts the source of formal privacy guarantees from post-hoc model-parameter perturbation (as in certified unlearning) to a data pre-protection step applied before training. A privacy guarantee immune to post-processing (e.g., probabilistic k-anonymity or epsilon-DP) is applied to the training data D to produce protected data D'; a base model M' is trained on D'; M' is then fine-tuned on the full original data D for production use. Upon a forgetting request for a subset Df, the system rolls back to the protected base model M' (discarding the production model) and fine-tunes only on the retained data Dr = D\Df, so the released model's dependence on Df flows only through the pre-protected M', inheriting its post-processing-immune guarantee. The authors instantiate the framework with two privacy models (probabilistic k-anonymity via MDAV microaggregation + Anatomy-style permutation, and epsilon-DP via Laplace/exponential mechanisms), give formal propositions that the resulting unlearned model satisfies the chosen guarantee for the forgotten records (Propositions 1 and 2), and evaluate on four datasets (Adult, Heart, Credit, CIFAR10) with MLP, XGBoost, and ResNet18 models against exact unlearning (retrain-from-scratch, SISA) and a certified baseline (Certified-SP / Koloskova et al.).
threat_model: The paper states an explicit threat model (Section 4, 'Threat model'): an adversary whose goal is to infer membership in the forget set Df or reconstruct information about forgotten records, by observing or querying the post-unlearning model M'_Dr in either white-box or black-box settings. The adversary is NOT assumed to have access to the pre-unlearning production model M'_D or any intermediate artifact, and what the adversary may have already inferred before the unlearning request is explicitly out of scope (matching the scope typically assumed by retraining-from-scratch and other certified/exact baselines). The framework also relies on a stated assumption, called 'no known dependencies between forgotten and retained data' -- the adversary is assumed unable to exploit correlations between Df and Dr to reconstruct information about Df from Dr -- which the authors note pervades (often implicitly) the wider unlearning literature. Privacy protection is defined data-level (probabilistic k-anonymity bounds re-identification probability at 1/k; epsilon-DP bounds output distinguishability by exp(epsilon)), not model-level.
limitations: The formal privacy guarantee is inherited via post-processing immunity plus an explicit, admittedly literature-wide but strong assumption of 'no known dependencies between forgotten and retained data' -- if this assumption is violated (e.g., correlated or duplicated records split across Df and Dr), the stated guarantee for Df may not hold, and the paper places responsibility for avoiding such dependencies on the model manager rather than providing a mechanism to detect or prevent them. The initial data-protection and pre-training stage is described as the most computationally expensive part of the framework (a one-time cost not amortized against the efficiency numbers per forgetting-request comparison, though the paper argues it is amortized over many future requests). Utility loss from data pre-protection is real and instantiation-dependent: EUPG(eps=1) showed a larger utility drop than EUPG(k=30) on XGBoost/Heart and lower CIFAR10 accuracy (83.17% vs 85.40% for k=30), and the paper's own analysis attributes XGBoost degradation to an inherent limitation of the two-stage protect-then-fine-tune design (fine-tuning only updates leaf values, so suboptimal splits from protected pre-training are not correctable). The threat model explicitly excludes any inference the adversary makes before the unlearning request and excludes access to the pre-unlearning production model or intermediate artifacts, which narrows the guarantee's real-world coverage.

datasets_used: Adult income (32,561 train / 16,281 test records, tabular, binary income classification)
Heart disease / cardiovascular-disease dataset (55,869 train / 14,131 test records, tabular)
Credit information / GiveMeSomeCredit (96,215 train / 24,054 test records, tabular, 6.92% positive class)
CIFAR10 (60,000 32x32 RGB images, 10 classes, 50,000 train / 10,000 test)
quantitative_results: CIFAR10/ResNet18 post-unlearning accuracy: Retrain 94.25%, SISA 69.19%, Certified-SP 45.91%, EUPG(k=30) 85.40%, EUPG(eps=1) 83.17%
CIFAR10 pre-unlearning MIA AUC: Retrain 57.71, Certified-SP 57.71, EUPG(k=30) 49.64, EUPG(eps=1) 49.18
Neural-network unlearning speedup vs Retrain: EUPG(k=30) 14.76x, EUPG(eps=1) 14.84x, Certified-SP 14.04x, SISA 0.62x
XGBoost unlearning speedup vs Retrain: EUPG(k=30) 2.74x, EUPG(eps=1) 2.46x, SISA 0.33x (times ~0.030-0.048s)
Adult/MLP pre-unlearning MIA AUC/TPR@1%FPR: Retrain 55.49/3.78, EUPG(k=30) 51.48/1.54, EUPG(eps=1) 51.35/1.43; post-unlearning all methods converge near ~49.7-50.0 MIA AUC
Heart/XGBoost pre-unlearning MIA AUC: Retrain 64.58, SISA 60.28, EUPG(k=30) 51.51, EUPG(eps=1) 51.36
k=30 corresponds to re-identification probability bound 1/k ~ 0.033, below the 0.09 threshold recommended by the European Medicines Agency and Health Canada for clinical data
baselines_compared: Exact unlearning: full retraining from scratch
Exact unlearning: SISA (5 shards x 10 slices)
Certified unlearning: Certified-SP (Koloskova et al., privacy amplification by stochastic post-processing, gradient-clipping variant, epsilon=1/delta=1e-5; not applicable to XGBoost)
